title: Lesson 2: Who You Are - Identity in Jesus the Son
description: You are made in the image of God and adopted through Jesus the Son - the second person of the Trinity. This lesson roots your identity not in performance or approval but in the finished work of Christ, who restores the image that sin cracked and makes you a full child of the Father.

blocks.text: In the last lesson, you encountered God the Father - the One who runs toward you, who delights in you, and who loves you before you ever love Him back. Now it is time to meet God the Son, and to understand what Jesus did not just for you but to you. The cross is not merely a transaction that cleared your debt. It is the moment your identity was completely changed.
The Search That Never Ends

A.W. Tozer once wrote, "What comes into our minds when we think about God is the most important thing about us." That is a striking claim - not what you do, not what you have accomplished, but what you think about God. He was pointing to something deep: the way we see God shapes the way we see ourselves, and the way we see ourselves shapes everything else.

If you look honestly at human life, one pattern shows up everywhere. People are searching. For significance. For belonging. For something that will finally feel like enough. We try relationships, achievements, experiences - and none of them quite deliver. We feel it in the quiet moments, the nagging sense that we were made for something more than what we have found.
That restlessness is not a malfunction. It is a clue. You were made for something specific, and until you find it, nothing else will fully satisfy.
What is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him? Yet you have made him a little lower than the heavenly beings and crowned him with glory and honor.
2.1 - What comes to mind when you think about who God is? And honestly, what does that reveal about how you see yourself?
Made to Reflect

The first pages of Scripture make a claim that changes everything: you are made in the image of God. This is not just a theological footnote. It is the most foundational truth about every human being who has ever lived.
So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them.

Think of it this way: imagine the glory of God is straight above us, blinding and beautiful. God designed humans to be mirrors set at a 45-degree angle - so when people see other people living in step with God, they catch a glimpse of Him. You are not just a person living a life. You are a walking, breathing reflection of the One who holds all things together. His endless power, love, and purpose - designed into you, displayed through you.

That is the original design. You were made to image - not just to exist as an image-bearer but to actively display, reflect, and point people toward who God is. This explains the deep joy we feel when we live close to God. We are most fully ourselves when we are most fully reflecting Him.
2.2 - What does it mean to you personally that you were made to reflect God? How does that change the way you see your ordinary, everyday life?
The Mirror We Cracked

But you already know what happened. When the first humans turned away from God, they cracked the mirror. The image was not destroyed - every human still bears it - but it was distorted. Instead of reflecting God's glory outward, we turned inward. We began chasing the glory that used to be ours through God by looking for it in other places.

Approval. Status. Control. Comfort. Beauty. Performance. These are not evil things in themselves. But we have a way of making them into the thing that defines us - the thing we cannot lose without losing ourselves. And none of them are big enough to hold the weight of an identity. They all eventually crack under the pressure.

This is why performance-based Christianity is so exhausting. When you are trying to earn your standing with God, you are essentially trying to manufacture what only He can give. The treadmill never stops. There is always one more thing to do, one more standard to reach, one more reason to doubt whether you are really enough.
2.3 - Where in your own life have you looked for significance, approval, or belonging in things that could not actually deliver it? What did that feel like?
Before the Work Begins

Here is a moment from the life of Jesus that changes everything. Before He preached a single sermon, healed a single person, or performed a single miracle - before His public ministry began at all - the Father spoke over Him at His baptism:

And when Jesus was baptized, immediately he went up from the water, and behold, the heavens were opened to him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and coming to rest on him; and behold, a voice from heaven said, "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased."

Identity declared before performance. Beloved before useful. The Father called Jesus His beloved Son before a single miracle, before a single sermon, before a single act of ministry. Approval came first. The work flowed from the identity, not the other way around.

And here is the stunning thing: what the Father declared over Jesus, He declares over you through Jesus. You are not earning your way into His approval. The Father looked at you while you were still far off and called you beloved. The identity comes first. The life flows from there.
2.4 - The Father called Jesus "beloved" before any public ministry. How does that reframe the way you think about your own worth before God - before your performance, your record, or your successes?
What Jesus Actually Did

There is no other event in history more confusing at first look than the death of Jesus. He was a sinless man - falsely charged by a mob, tortured, and crucified as a criminal. How does this make any sense? But what if there is a greater mission? What if all this injustice was actually reallocated justice?

God wanted to display His glory, His value, His love, so brightly that the whole world could understand how much He wants to pursue sinful people. So Jesus became the bridge between sinful humanity and perfect God. Your guilt was placed on Him. His righteousness was placed on you. That is the exchange. Man gets to enjoy restored relationship with his Creator, and God is known as the Sacrificial Hero who offers salvation to all who receive it.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.

This single verse describes one of the most staggering transactions in history. He took your sin. You received His righteousness. The cross does not just forgive you - it exchanges you. What you were, He took on. What He was, He gives to you. This is what theologians call the great exchange.
Adopted, Not Just Forgiven

Here is where the gospel goes further than most people realize. When Jesus brings you back to God, He does not just clear your record. He changes your name. You are not simply a forgiven sinner. You are an adopted child.
See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are.
He predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will.

John stops to marvel at this. "See what kind of love" - as if he is still stunned by it after years of thinking about it. The love that makes you a child of God is not ordinary love. It is the kind that requires you to pause and really look at it.

Adoption in the ancient world meant full legal standing in the family. Not a second-tier member, not a guest. A son. A daughter. With all the rights, belonging, and inheritance that came with it. That is the language the New Testament uses for what God has done for you in Christ. You did not work your way into the family. You were brought in by the Son.

2.5 - What is the difference between living as a forgiven sinner trying to do better versus living as an adopted child who is already fully loved? What would shift in your daily life if you really believed the second one?
The Identity Exchange
Here is a simple way to see what Jesus accomplished. Think of three columns - what you had before, and what you have now:

items: Guilty in the Fall - but now Justified in the Son. Your record is sealed by His.
Orphan in the Fall - but now Adopted in the Son. You have a Father who chose you.
Dead in the Fall - but now Alive in the Son. A new life with a new nature.

blocks.text: Tim Keller once put it this way: "Religion says: I obey, therefore I am accepted. The gospel says: I am accepted, therefore I obey." Religion runs on the first engine. The gospel runs on the second. This is the new operating system. You do not obey to be adopted. You obey because you are.

And as the mirror is being restored through Christ - as you grow closer to Him day by day - you increasingly reflect what you were always designed to reflect. Not because you are trying harder but because the Vine is flowing through the branch.
And we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another. For this comes from the Lord who is the Spirit.
Living From a Settled Identity

When your identity is settled in God through Christ, something interesting happens. You stop needing the world to tell you who you are. You stop performing for approval because you already have it. You stop chasing counterfeit glories because you have access to the real one.

John Piper describes this as Christian Hedonism - the idea that God is most glorified in us when we are most satisfied in Him. A satisfied child does not strain and strive. A satisfied child lives from a full place, not an empty one. And that fullness naturally overflows. When you are genuinely satisfied in God, you reflect His image more clearly to the people around you - not because you are trying harder, but because you are drawing from the right source.

Think of one person in your life right now - a family member, a coworker, a neighbor. That person is an image-bearer too, whether they know it or not. How you treat them is a reflection of how you understand who God is. The way you love, listen, forgive, and serve is either pointing them toward the Father or it is not.
2.6 - Where are you still trying to earn what Jesus already secured? Where in your daily life is your sense of worth most dependent on your performance or other people's approval?
2.7 - What would it look like for you to be so satisfied in God this week that it naturally changed how you showed up for the people around you? Write one specific, concrete picture.
This Week's Practice

Each morning this week, read 1 John 3:1 slowly: "See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God - and so we are." Then sit with one question before your day begins: If I am already fully adopted and fully loved, what do I not have to prove today? Let that question move with you through the day.

Also this week, read Ephesians 1 in one sitting. Watch for how many times Paul uses the phrase "in Christ" or "in him." Every blessing he describes - chosen, adopted, forgiven, sealed - is yours because of your union with Jesus. You are not trying to get something from God. You already have it. The task is learning to believe it.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
